text:
The circle is the most satisfying shape, and I want to make a genuine case for this rather than just defaulting to the obvious answer.

What makes the circle extraordinary is that it resolves a tension that no other shape even attempts. Every other shape is a negotiation, a compromise between competing directions. A square insists on four emphatic decisions. A triangle forces three corners where energy accumulates and then stops, frustrated. Even the most elegant polygon is ultimately a collection of interruptions. But the circle refuses to interrupt itself. It commits fully to a single organizing principle, equidistance from a center, and follows that principle without exception or hesitation all the way around until it arrives exactly where it began. It is the only shape that is also a journey that ends where it starts, which is not a failure to go anywhere but rather a profound kind of completion.

There is also something deeply satisfying about the relationship between the circle and the infinite. Pi goes on forever, irrational and transcendent, yet the circle that generates it looks like the simplest thing in the world. You can draw one with a stick tied to a rope. Children draw them instinctively. And yet nested within that humble form is one of the most mysterious numbers in mathematics. The circle hides its complexity behind perfect accessibility, which is the mark of real sophistication.

Physically, the circle also wins. It is how ripples propagate through water because disturbance spreads equally in all directions. It is how planets orbit, roughly. It is the cross-section of the strongest beams and pipes, because a circle distributes stress with no weak angles, no points where force concentrates unfairly. Nature reaches for the circle when it needs something to endure.

But perhaps most satisfying of all is the feeling of closing a circle, in the figurative sense we borrowed from geometry precisely because the geometry earns it. When something comes full circle, we mean it has found its resolution. The circle gave us our best metaphor for completion because its form embodies completion better than any other shape can.
